statement.markdown: Given the preorder traversal of a binary search tree (BST), construct the BST and return its postorder traversal.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the BST.
The next N lines contain the elements of the preorder traversal.
statement.outputFormat: A single line containing the postorder traversal of the constructed BST, with node values separated by spaces.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the BST.

examples.explanation: The preorder traversal [40, 10, 20, 30, 60, 50] constructs a BST whose postorder traversal is 30 20 10 50 60 40.
Preorder [8, 5, 10] constructs a BST with root 8, left child 5, right child 10. Postorder traversal is 5 10 8.

hints.text: The preorder traversal visits the root node first, then the left subtree, and finally the right subtree. This means the first element in the preorder array is the root of the BST.

In a BST, all nodes to the left of a node have values less than the node, and all nodes to the right have values greater than the node. You can use this property to identify the left and right subtrees from the preorder array.

You can recursively construct the left and right subtrees by finding the elements in the preorder array that are less than and greater than the current root node, respectively. The preorder array is already given in a way that it can be divided into left and right subtrees based on the value of the root.

editorial.title: Constructing a Binary Search Tree from Preorder Traversal
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by utilizing the properties of a Binary Search Tree (BST), where for every node, all elements in the left subtree are less than the node, and all elements in the right subtree are greater. This property can guide the construction of the tree from the preorder traversal.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible combinations of constructing the tree, which would be highly inefficient due to the large number of possible trees. However, this approach is correct but slow due to its exponential time complexity.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves using a recursive approach that takes advantage of the BST property to efficiently construct the tree. By maintaining a lower and upper bound for each recursive call, we can ensure that the tree is constructed correctly.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution utilizes a recursive function that constructs the BST by iteratively popping elements from the preorder traversal and adding them to the tree, ensuring that the BST property is maintained at each step. This approach has a time complexity of O(N) and a space complexity of O(N), making it efficient for large inputs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction, showing that the recursive function correctly constructs the BST by maintaining the BST property at each step. The base case is when the preorder traversal is empty, in which case the function returns an empty tree. The inductive step involves showing that if the function correctly constructs the tree for a given preorder traversal, it will also correctly construct the tree for the remaining elements.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm can be performed by manually tracing the execution of the function on a sample input, verifying that the tree is constructed correctly and that the BST property is maintained at each step.
commonMistakes: Not maintaining the BST property during tree construction
Incorrectly handling the base case of the recursion
Failing to update the lower and upper bounds correctly
Not properly popping elements from the preorder traversal
Not handling the case where the input preorder traversal is empty
